text: Laparoscopic Ventral Mesh Rectopexy Step by step, rectal prolapse is defined as protrusion of the entire thickness of the rectal wall through the anal sphincter. Classified into two types, mucosal prolapse and full thickness prolapse. Multiple risk factor is defined. The most important risk are chronic straining with defecation and constipation. The aim of treatment for rectal prolapse are Control prolapse and prevent recurrence.

Restore normal bowel function in term of continence and constipation. Surgery can be split into two main approaches. Pirineal. And abdominal. Over one hundred procedure now described. But now there is no established consensus as to the best surgical treatment.

Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy. This procedure aims to correct the descent of the posterior and middle pelvic compartments, combined with reinforcement of the rectovaginal septum. The operation has been shown to have low recurrence rate and also improves incontinence and constipation. The advantage of the laparoscopic approach are shorter hospital stay, quicker patient recovery, lower costs and less morbidity.

No history of constipation and incontinence. Gastroscope and colonoscope reveal normal. No staining evaluation in this case. Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is performed. Step-up procedure are shown in this slide.

The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position with arms tucked. The surgical team is arranged with the surgeon and camera operator on the right side and the assistant on the left side. The camera is inserted through the umbilical port, and three additional 5mm ports are placed for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the right ureter crossing the right iliac artery. The assistant retracts the mesosigmoid ventrally, while the surgeon makes a peritoneal incision over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon exposes the sacral hollow using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, preparing the area for subsequent mesh fixation.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the right hypogastric nerve while opening the rectovaginal septum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon dissects between the rectum and the posterior vaginal wall, preserving the lateral ligament to prevent recurrence and reduce constipation risk. The rectovaginal septum is opened widely down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision caudally into an inverted J shape along the right side of the rectum, curving to the left over the ventral aspect of the rectum and the pouch of Douglas. A strip of prolene mesh, trimmed to three by fifteen centimeters, is inserted. Temporary hysteropexy is achieved using transparietal sutures, suspending the uterus to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable sutures to attach the mesh to the ventral aspect on both sides of the distal rectum, securing it in place.

The surgeon positions the mesh to reinforce the rectovaginal septum, securing it to the sacral promontory with a tacker. The lateral borders of the incised peritoneum are then closed over the mesh using resorbable sutures.

The mesh should be covered completely with peritoneum to avoid any later small bowel fixation to the mesh. There was no complication post-surgery. Patient can discharge on post-operative day four. There was no recurrent on four-month follow-up. No new developed constipation or incontinence.

In conclusion, a variety of procedures is available for correction of rectal prolapse. Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy appears to be a safe and effective surgical option for folicness rectal prolapse, in terms of intraoperative, postoperative, and mesh-related complications. with acceptable recurrence rate and satisfactory improvement of functional outcome Thank you for your attention
